No Poverty.reason: The course explicitly covers 'Socio-Economic Rights' (Week 12), which directly relates to social protection and economic resources for the poor and vulnerable, aligning with Targets 1.3 and 1.4.

Zero Hunger.reason: The 'Indigenous Communities Members of the Lhaka Honhat Association v. Argentina' case (Week 13) often involves land rights and traditional livelihoods, which can be inferred to impact food access and security for indigenous peoples (Target 2.3).
evidence: Indigenous Communities Members of the Lhaka Honhat Association v. Argentina

Good Health and Well-being.reason: No explicit or strong inferred evidence in the syllabus directly addresses health services, disease prevention, or specific well-being targets of SDG 3.

Quality Education.reason: The course directly contributes to Target 4.7 by providing education on human rights and fostering 'global citizenship,' which are explicit components of sustainable development education.

Gender Equality.reason: While not a primary focus, International Human Rights Law inherently addresses non-discrimination, which includes gender equality (Target 5.1). This is an underlying principle rather than an explicit topic.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: The 'Indigenous Communities Members of the Lhaka Honhat Association v. Argentina' case (Week 13) often addresses rights to natural resources, which can be inferred to include access to clean water (Target 6.1).
evidence: Indigenous Communities Members of the Lhaka Honhat Association v. Argentina

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: There is no explicit or inferred evidence in the course syllabus related to affordable energy, clean energy, or energy efficiency.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: Week 10 explicitly covers 'Modern Slavery, Business & Human Rights,' directly addressing Targets 8.7 (ending modern slavery and forced labor) and 8.8 (protecting labor rights).

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: No explicit or strong inferred evidence in the syllabus relates to industry, innovation, infrastructure development, or technological upgrading.

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course extensively covers topics like 'Racial Discrimination,' 'Non-Refoulement Principle,' and 'Indigenous Communities' rights, which are fundamental to promoting inclusion and eliminating discriminatory practices, directly addressing Targets 10.2, 10.3, and 10.7.
evidence: Indigenous Communities Members of the Lhaka Honhat Association v. Argentina

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: The 'Mohamed Ben Djazia and Naouel Bellili v. Spain' case (Week 12) from CESCR often addresses housing rights, which directly links to Target 11.1 (access to adequate, safe, and affordable housing).
evidence: CESCR, Mohamed Ben Djazia and Naouel Bellili v. Spain

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The topic 'Business & Human Rights' (Week 10) can be weakly inferred to encourage responsible corporate practices, indirectly touching upon Target 12.6 (sustainable practices in companies).

Climate Action.reason: Week 13 is dedicated to 'Climate, Environment & Human Rights,' including landmark cases like 'KlimaSeniorinnen Schweiz' and 'Teitiota v. New Zealand,' directly addressing climate change impacts and legal responses (Targets 13.1, 13.2, 13.3).

Life Below Water.reason: No explicit or strong inferred evidence in the syllabus relates to marine ecosystems, ocean health, or sustainable fishing practices.

Life on Land.reason: The topic 'Climate, Environment & Human Rights' (Week 13) and the 'Indigenous Communities' case directly relate to the sustainable use and conservation of terrestrial ecosystems and land rights (Target 15.1).
evidence: Indigenous Communities Members of the Lhaka Honhat Association v. Argentina

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: This SDG is the core of the course, focusing entirely on 'International Laws of Human Right,' 'Human Rights Monitoring Systems,' and promoting 'peace, democracy, and the rule of law' (Targets 16.3, 16.6, 16.10).
evidence: Human Rights Monitoring System of the United Nations
peace, democracy, and the rule of law

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course extensively covers various 'Human Rights Monitoring Systems' (UN, European, Inter-American, African), demonstrating international cooperation, institutional strengthening, and policy coherence essential for global partnerships (Targets 17.9, 17.14, 17.16).
evidence: Human Rights Monitoring System of the United Nations
